title: IR Program Metrics and KPIs: MTTD, MTTR, and Tracking
content:
## Purpose

This guide defines the key metrics and KPIs for measuring the effectiveness of the incident response program. Metrics provide objective evidence of program maturity, identify areas for improvement, justify resource requests, and enable benchmarking against industry standards. Without measurement, IR programs operate on intuition rather than evidence.

## Scope

Applies to the measurement and reporting of all incident response activities, from initial detection through post-incident review. Covers operational metrics (how fast and well we respond), programmatic metrics (how mature and effective the program is), and business-aligned metrics (how IR protects the business).

## Core IR Metrics Definitions

### Time-Based Metrics

These metrics track the speed of response across the incident lifecycle. Each metric measures the elapsed time between two defined points.

| Metric | Full Name | Start Point | End Point | Target (Industry Benchmark) |
|--------|-----------|-------------|-----------|----------------------------|
| **MTTD** | Mean Time to Detect | Compromise occurs | Security team becomes aware | < 24 hours (median 16 days per IBM) |
| **MTTA** | Mean Time to Acknowledge | Alert fires | Analyst begins investigation | < 15 minutes for P1, < 1 hour for P2 |
| **MTTI** | Mean Time to Investigate | Investigation begins | Root cause identified | < 4 hours for P1, < 24 hours for P2 |
| **MTTC** | Mean Time to Contain | Containment decision made | Containment actions completed | < 1 hour for P1, < 4 hours for P2 |
| **MTTR** | Mean Time to Recover | Recovery begins | Normal operations restored | < 24 hours for P1, < 72 hours for P2 |
| **MTTRE** | Mean Time to Remediate (End-to-End) | Compromise occurs | Incident fully closed | Varies by severity; track trend |
| **Dwell Time** | Attacker Dwell Time | Initial compromise | Detection | < 24 hours (goal); industry median varies widely |

### How to Calculate Time-Based Metrics

1. **For each incident**, record timestamps at each lifecycle stage in the incident management platform.
2. **Calculate individual incident times** by subtracting the start timestamp from the end timestamp.
3. **Calculate the mean** across all incidents in the reporting period, segmented by severity.
4. **Calculate the median** in addition to the mean -- medians are more resistant to outlier skew from single extreme incidents.
5. **Track percentiles** (P50, P90, P95) for a fuller picture of distribution.

```
Example calculation:

Incident #1: MTTD = 2 hours
Incident #2: MTTD = 45 minutes
Incident #3: MTTD = 6 hours
Incident #4: MTTD = 1 hour
Incident #5: MTTD = 30 minutes

Mean MTTD = (2 + 0.75 + 6 + 1 + 0.5) / 5 = 2.05 hours
Median MTTD = 1 hour (middle value when sorted)
P90 MTTD = 6 hours (90th percentile -- 90% of incidents detected faster than this)
```

### Volume and Classification Metrics

| Metric | Definition | Why It Matters |
|--------|-----------|----------------|
| Total incidents per period | Count of confirmed incidents by severity, type, and source | Trend analysis, resource planning |
| Alert-to-incident ratio | Number of alerts investigated vs. confirmed as incidents | Measures detection quality and alert fatigue |
| False positive rate | Percentage of investigated alerts that are not incidents | High FP rate indicates tuning needed |
| Escalation rate | Percentage of incidents escalated from Tier 1 to Tier 2/3 | Measures Tier 1 capability and detection rule quality |
| Repeat incidents | Incidents with same root cause as a previous incident | Measures effectiveness of remediation and lessons learned |
| Incidents by attack vector | Breakdown by initial access method (phishing, exploitation, credential abuse) | Guides preventive investment |
| Incidents by detection source | Which tools or processes detected the incident (SIEM, EDR, user report, TI) | Measures detection coverage and identifies blind spots |

### Quality and Effectiveness Metrics

| Metric | Definition | Why It Matters |
|--------|-----------|----------------|
| Containment effectiveness | Percentage of incidents where attacker activity stopped after containment | Measures whether containment actions actually work |
| Re-compromise rate | Percentage of incidents where the same system or vector is re-compromised within 90 days | Measures eradication and hardening effectiveness |
| Post-incident action completion rate | Percentage of post-incident review recommendations implemented within the target timeframe | Measures organizational learning and follow-through |
| Evidence preservation compliance | Percentage of incidents where evidence was collected per the Evidence Preservation SOP | Measures forensic readiness |
| Handoff quality score | Rating of handoff completeness (checklist compliance) | Measures operational continuity |
| SLA compliance rate | Percentage of incidents meeting response time SLAs by severity level | Measures operational performance against commitments |

### Business-Aligned Metrics

| Metric | Definition | Audience |
|--------|-----------|----------|
| Business downtime from incidents | Total hours of service disruption caused by security incidents | Executive leadership, board |
| Financial impact | Direct costs (response, recovery, fines) and indirect costs (lost revenue, reputation) | CFO, board |
| Data records exposed | Number of records (PII, PHI, PCI, credentials) exposed in breaches | Privacy officer, regulators |
| Regulatory notification count | Number of required regulatory notifications filed | Legal, compliance |
| Customer impact | Number of customers affected by security incidents | Customer success, executive leadership |
| Insurance utilization | Claims filed, coverage used, premium impact | CFO, risk management |

## Reporting Framework

### Operational Dashboard (Real-Time, SOC-Facing)

Display on SOC monitors and update continuously:
- Open incidents by severity (current count)
- Current MTTA for open alerts
- Incidents opened and closed today/this week
- Alert queue depth and aging
- SLA compliance status (on track, at risk, breached)

### Monthly IR Report (Management-Facing)

Produce monthly and distribute to CISO and security leadership:

1. **Executive summary**: Key incidents, trends, and program health assessment (2-3 paragraphs).
2. **Metrics dashboard**:
   - MTTD, MTTA, MTTI, MTTC, MTTR -- current month, trend over 6 months, comparison to target.
   - Incident volume by severity and type.
   - Alert-to-incident ratio and false positive rate.
   - SLA compliance rates.
3. **Notable incidents**: Summary of significant incidents with lessons learned.
4. **Post-incident action status**: Tracking table for open remediation items from post-incident reviews.
5. **Recommendations**: Data-driven recommendations for program improvement.

### Quarterly Board Report (Executive/Board-Facing)

Produce quarterly for executive leadership and board of directors:

1. **Business risk posture**: How IR metrics translate to business risk.
2. **Trend analysis**: Quarter-over-quarter trends in key metrics.
3. **Benchmarking**: Comparison to industry benchmarks where available.
4. **Resource needs**: Data-driven justification for headcount, tools, or budget.
5. **Program maturity assessment**: Current maturity level and improvement roadmap.

## Setting Targets and Benchmarks

### Maturity-Based Targets

| Maturity Level | MTTD | MTTA (P1) | MTTC (P1) | MTTR (P1) | FP Rate |
|---------------|------|-----------|-----------|-----------|--------|
| Initial | Days-weeks | > 1 hour | > 8 hours | > 1 week | > 50% |
| Developing | 24-72 hours | 30-60 min | 4-8 hours | 3-5 days | 30-50% |
| Defined | 4-24 hours | 15-30 min | 1-4 hours | 1-3 days | 15-30% |
| Managed | 1-4 hours | 5-15 min | 30 min - 1 hour | 4-24 hours | 5-15% |
| Optimized | < 1 hour | < 5 min | < 30 min | < 4 hours | < 5% |

### How to Use Benchmarks

1. Start by measuring current state honestly. Do not set targets before establishing a baseline.
2. Set improvement targets that are achievable -- a 20-30% improvement per quarter is aggressive but realistic.
3. Benchmark against your own historical performance first. Industry benchmarks provide context but every organization is different.
4. Do not optimize a single metric in isolation. Improving MTTD at the expense of false positive rate (by lowering detection thresholds) is counterproductive.

## Data Collection and Tooling

### Required Data Points per Incident

To calculate all metrics above, the following timestamps and data must be recorded for every incident:

| Data Point | When to Record | Field in Incident Ticket |
|-----------|---------------|-------------------------|
| Compromise timestamp | During investigation (may be estimated) | `compromise_time` |
| Detection timestamp | When the alert fired or the incident was reported | `detection_time` |
| Acknowledgment timestamp | When an analyst began working the alert | `acknowledgment_time` |
| Investigation start | When active investigation began | `investigation_start` |
| Root cause identified | When the root cause was determined | `root_cause_time` |
| Containment start | When containment actions began | `containment_start` |
| Containment complete | When containment was verified effective | `containment_complete` |
| Recovery start | When recovery/rebuild began | `recovery_start` |
| Recovery complete | When normal operations restored | `recovery_complete` |
| Incident closed | When all actions complete and incident closed | `closed_time` |
| Severity | Severity classification | `severity` |
| Incident type | Attack type / category | `incident_type` |
| Detection source | Tool or process that detected | `detection_source` |
| Attack vector | Initial access method | `attack_vector` |
| Systems affected | Count and list of affected assets | `affected_assets` |
| Data exposure | Whether data was accessed/exfiltrated and type | `data_exposure` |

### Automating Metric Collection

1. Configure the incident management platform to require timestamps at each lifecycle stage before allowing status transitions.
2. Build automated dashboards that pull from incident ticket data -- do not rely on manual spreadsheet compilation.
3. Integrate SIEM alert timestamps with incident ticket timestamps to calculate MTTD and MTTA automatically.
4. Schedule automated monthly report generation with trend calculations.
5. Implement data quality checks to flag incidents with missing or inconsistent timestamps.

## Common Pitfalls

| Pitfall | Why It Happens | How to Avoid |
|---------|---------------|---------------|
| Measuring too many metrics | Enthusiasm for data without focus | Start with 5-7 core metrics, add more as maturity increases |
| Metrics without context | Numbers without narrative | Always pair metrics with analysis and explanation |
| Gaming metrics | Pressure to hit targets leads to misclassification | Audit a sample of incidents quarterly for accuracy |
| Ignoring outliers | Mean hides extreme cases | Report percentiles alongside means |
| Inconsistent definitions | Different analysts define "containment" differently | Document precise definitions and train the team |
| No baseline | Setting targets without knowing current state | Measure for 3 months before setting improvement targets |
| Static targets | Targets never updated as the program matures | Review and adjust targets quarterly |

## Escalation Criteria

- MTTD or MTTR trends upward for 3 consecutive months: Escalate to CISO with root cause analysis and resource request.
- SLA compliance drops below 80% for any severity level: Escalate to SOC Manager for staffing and process review.
- False positive rate exceeds 40%: Escalate to detection engineering for alert tuning initiative.
- Post-incident action completion rate drops below 60%: Escalate to CISO for organizational accountability enforcement.
- Repeat incident rate exceeds 15%: Escalate to CISO -- remediation process is failing.

## References

- NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2 -- Incident Handling, Section 3.4 (Incident Handling Checklist and Metrics)
- NIST Cybersecurity Framework -- Respond and Recover Functions
- SANS Incident Response Metrics -- Key Performance Indicators
- IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report (annual)
- Verizon Data Breach Investigations Report (DBIR) -- annual benchmarking data
- MITRE ATT&CK -- Detection and Response Coverage Assessment
